This practice is at odds with established consumer protection laws, including directive 93/13/EEC, which prohibits unfair terms.

paragraphs.text: I sincerely request that the commission consider adding provisions to address the artificial destruction of purchased digital content, such as software, particularly when these products are not sold as a service. There is a growing trend of offering digital content without clear expiration dates or ongoing fees, which creates the illusion of a perpetual licensesomething that consumers typically expect. Unfortunately, the license agreements often include stringent terms that permit sellers to revoke licenses at any time, for "any or no reason." This practice is at odds with established consumer protection laws, including directive 93/13/EEC, which prohibits unfair terms. Many consumers are unaware of these stipulations, and those who do know often find it difficult to make informed decisions due to the ambiguous language. In practice, these terms allow intellectual property holders to unfairly strip customers of their purchases. This situation is often presented as "ending support," where the digital content is engineered so that once support is withdrawn, the product becomes completely non-functional, reflecting programmed obsolescence rather than a digital equivalent of wear and tear. In the context of video games, the recent European Citizens' Initiative called "Stop Destroying Videogames" has amassed over 1.4 million signatures, underscoring the pressing need to tackle the destruction of sold products. Considering that the Digital Fairness Act seeks to eliminate practices that are detrimental to consumers and to strengthen online consumer protections, I strongly urge the commission to take action on this matter to ensure that digital products sold to consumers cannot be arbitrarily taken away. Thank you for your dedication to this important issue.
